caveats: a: The BLS occupation population includes all NAICS 314900, including 314994 and consumer-product plants outside the narrowed lens.
a: Headcount shares are converted to wage exposure judgmentally; higher-paid commercial and management work raises the wage-weighted share.
a: Some embroidery digitization and production automation is already deployed, so only not-yet-automated work is included.
rho: Neither survey isolates US NAICS 314999 or the narrowed contract-service subset.
rho: Self-reported adoption does not establish sustained labor savings.
rho: The estimate excludes price, demand, and valuation effects.

e: Several listed products can be sold through either recurring B2B supply relationships or consumer brands, so website classification may be ambiguous.
e: The injected LMM firm count is itself an estimate and should not be converted into a claimed observed count.

s5: Age 55-plus is a broad band and does not measure sale intent or timing.
s5: Founder dependence can turn succession events into closures rather than transferable operations.

q: Raw-material and freight movements make labor-benefit pass-through difficult to isolate from invoice prices.
q: Retention varies materially between standardized bulk work and qualification-heavy custom production.

d5: Domestic output can decline through import substitution even when US end demand is stable.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: All Other Miscellaneous Textile Product Mills

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B job, batch, or supply-order revenue, often priced per unit or per production run under quotes, purchase orders, and recurring customer programs; customers outsource specialized labor, equipment, quality control, and fulfillment.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 314999 combines contract or fee textile work with unrelated consumer and industrial product manufacturing. To keep one coherent outsourced-service screen, the lens retains recurring contract converters and specification-driven B2B producers and excludes primarily branded consumer-goods manufacturers.
lens.excluded_activities: Consumer-branded sleeping-bag, diaper, dust-cloth, or similar finished-goods manufacturers whose economics are primarily brand and retail product sales; captive units, shells, non-control financing vehicles, one-off artisans, and operations without transferable customer and production systems.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing repeat contract textile conversion, embellishment, cutting, binding, sewing, and custom or specification-driven industrial textile products to external commercial customers, including embroidery-for-trade and repeat-production technical-textile work.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations at NAICS 314900 rather than the narrowed 314999 contract-conversion lens, and it measures employment rather than task exposure. I assigned substantial partial exposure to quoting, design preparation, order entry, customer service, procurement, scheduling, billing, general office work, quality documentation, and visual inspection, while assigning much less to sewing, machine tending, maintenance, packing, and material handling. The proxy may understate exposure for design-heavy embroidery contractors and overstate it for labor-intensive sewn technical products.
caveats: The BLS occupation population includes all NAICS 314900, including 314994 and consumer-product plants outside the narrowed lens.
Headcount shares are converted to wage exposure judgmentally; higher-paid commercial and management work raises the wage-weighted share.
Some embroidery digitization and production automation is already deployed, so only not-yet-automated work is included.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task census across 15 US contract embroidery, textile-conversion, fire-hose, fishing-net, and sewn technical-product plants, recording current automation and time spent on design digitization, estimating, order administration, inspection, machine operation, and rework.
a.rationale: The closest BLS occupation table reports 61.92% production employment but also 11.57% office and administrative support, 5.11% sales, 5.64% management, 2.58% business and financial operations, and 1.78% design/media roles. Custom contract work creates document- and design-heavy tasks around each physical job, while AI vision can assist inspection, but most sewing and handling remains embodied work.

d5.bridge: BLS reports a 1987-2024 real-output trend for four-digit NAICS 3149, while USTR discusses the broader US textile and apparel supply chain. I compound the BLS 3149 annual output decline of 1.2% for five years to center near 0.94, then widen for the narrowed lens: contract embellishment and consumer-linked work may decline faster with imports, while medical, protective, specialty, industrial, and reshored short-run work may grow. The broad historic mix likely understates demand for advanced technical niches but may overstate demand for easily imported labor-intensive work.
caveats: The BLS output trend is at NAICS 3149, not 314999 or the narrowed service lens.
The retained basket spans cyclical consumer decoration and resilient technical or safety applications.
Domestic output can decline through import substitution even when US end demand is stable.
d5.change_evidence: Product-level real shipment and import series for embroidery-for-trade, fishing nets, fire hose, batting, weatherstripping, sleeping bags, and related contract conversion, combined with customer-industry forecasts and a panel of retained-lens firms' constant-price order volumes.
d5.rationale: BLS reports other textile product mill real output falling 1.2% annually over 1987-2024. USTR notes growth in US advanced textile categories such as medical, protective, specialty, industrial, and nonwoven products despite increasing finished-product imports, while reduced domestic apparel production lowers demand for US textile inputs. The retained mixed contract/technical basket therefore has a flat-to-modestly-declining center and wide dispersion.

caveats: No public dataset identifies what share of six-digit firms use the retained contract and B2B model.
Several listed products can be sold through either recurring B2B supply relationships or consumer brands, so website classification may be ambiguous.
The injected LMM firm count is itself an estimate and should not be converted into a claimed observed count.
e.change_evidence: Classify the 48 dataset-estimated LMM firms using D&B, Capital IQ, PitchBook, company websites, and customer interviews by contract-versus-branded revenue, repeat-order share, customer type, captive status, and standalone management.
e.rationale: The official industry definition spans contract embroidery and other art needlework for the trade as well as manufacturers of fishing nets, sleeping bags, textile fire hose, batting, dust cloths, and weatherstripping. The narrowed lens captures recurring contract converters and specification-driven B2B producers but excludes much of the branded consumer-product population, captive operations, and nontransferable shops.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 314999 ($1,459,034,313) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 314999 ($4,969,381,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $172,890 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 314999); class <5: 1,330 firms, avg revenue/firm $292,346 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $44,583 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 400 firms, avg revenue/firm $793,005 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $120,933 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 251 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,816,554 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $277,024 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 215 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,437,367 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $829,199 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 48 firms, avg revenue/firm $24,136,208 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,680,772 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 16 firms, avg revenue/firm $92,489,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $14,104,649 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 48

caveats: The value is inferred from the physical nature of the work rather than directly observed.
Import substitution removes demand from US lens operators even though a physical operator remains abroad.
Digital artwork creation can be self-served without eliminating the downstream production operator.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews on insourcing and self-service digitization, utilization data from automated embroidery and vision platforms, and case studies of lights-out textile conversion showing which responsibilities remain with a legally and commercially accountable operator.
o.rationale: Customers still need physical textiles to be cut, sewn, bound, embroidered, inspected, and delivered. Generative tools can create artwork or production files and automate coordination, but an accountable operator must translate specifications into material output, run and maintain equipment, control quality, and remedy failures. Some simple design work may become self-service, and customers may insource digital-to-machine production or source imports, leaving a small nonoperator residual.

caveats: No source directly measures five-year benefit retention for private contract textile firms.
Raw-material and freight movements make labor-benefit pass-through difficult to isolate from invoice prices.
Retention varies materially between standardized bulk work and qualification-heavy custom production.
q.change_evidence: Five years of customer-level pricing and contribution-margin data around automation projects, including quote frequency, rebid cadence, contract escalation terms, competitor win/loss notes, customer concentration, and separate results for contract embellishment and technical products.
q.rationale: Contract conversion is generally quoted at a fixed per-unit or per-job price, so an operator initially keeps labor savings. Repeat customers, rebids, and transparent production rates create eventual sharing, but design libraries, short runs, setup knowledge, quality consistency, switching friction, and service responsiveness can slow pass-through. Technical and safety-critical products should retain more than commoditized embroidery or simple sewn items.

rho.bridge: Auburn's 97-respondent cross-manufacturing survey and Zebra's global manufacturing-leader study are broader and likely larger-company-skewed. I translate their adoption stages, integration gaps, and stated barriers into five-year implementation of the exposed workflows in small contract textile firms. Their digital maturity likely overstates LMM readiness, while the bounded, repetitive nature of quoting, design, order, scheduling, and inspection tasks may make the narrowed lens easier to implement than the manufacturing average.
caveats: Neither survey isolates US NAICS 314999 or the narrowed contract-service subset.
Self-reported adoption does not establish sustained labor savings.
The estimate excludes price, demand, and valuation effects.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor cohort data and operator interviews covering installed embroidery digitization, ERP/job tracking, computer vision, scheduling, and customer-service systems at firms with under 250 employees, including time-to-value, failure rates, labor hours removed, and ongoing human-review requirements.
rho.rationale: Auburn found only 8% using AI in 2024, with 58% still at awareness or research, though the implementing share rose 12 percentage points. Zebra found only 16% had end-to-end real-time production visibility and reported integration obstacles. Five years should permit meaningful adoption of packaged design, workflow, and vision tools, but fragmented job data, legacy machines, quality requirements, and limited technical staff prevent full realization.

s5.bridge: Census measures women owners of employer firms across manufacturing and finds 62.4% age 55 or older, rather than transfer events among all owners of LMM contract textile firms. I treat age as succession pressure and apply a large haircut for owners who retain, close, transfer internally, or sell assets without a qualifying control transfer. The women-owner sample may be older than all owners and bias upward; small contract shops may also be more founder-dependent and close rather than transfer, biasing qualifying availability downward.
caveats: The source covers women owners across all manufacturing, not all owners or NAICS 314999.
Age 55-plus is a broad band and does not measure sale intent or timing.
Founder dependence can turn succession events into closures rather than transferable operations.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction and closure census for NAICS 314999 from PitchBook, Axial, GF Data, BizBuySell, broker files, and state business records, coded for control, buyer type, continuity, owner age, and failed sale processes.
s5.rationale: Older ownership in capital-intensive manufacturing makes succession a plausible source of deals, and contract textile shops often have founder relationships. However, an age distribution cannot establish transaction probability. The base assumes roughly one in four eligible firms transfers control within five years after excluding internal reorganizations, passive stakes, and noncontinuing closures.

principal_driver: Repeat custom workflows create unusually useful AI entry points around design, quoting, administration, scheduling, and inspection.
principal_weakness: The catch-all industry's heterogeneity makes eligibility, demand, and transfer incidence hard to measure reliably.
